coredata.dc:title: Study the variation of current density & corrosion behavior of modified M2 tool steel: Simulation & experimental
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ranjbar, Vahid
Tabrizi, Arvin Taghizadeh
Roostaei, Mohammad
Germchi, Sara Sheykh
Aghajani, Hossein
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the simulation of the current density was carried out through COMSOL Multiphysics on surface-modified M2 high-speed tool steel. The results demonstrated that in the presence of the nitride layer on the surface of M2, the current density was increased remarkably in the nitride layer and M2 was immune, and consequently, it can be predicted that the corrosion resistance was increased. For verification, the surface modification through plasma nitriding was applied on the M2 steel corrosion resistance and was evaluated through polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in the 3.5 wt% of NaCl solution. The results confirmed the simulation outcomes; therefore, optimization of the parameters was performed. The impression of the variation of the process parameter of plasma nitriding such as temperature (500, 550, and 600 °C), time (4, 6, and 8 h), and ratio of inserted gases (N2/H2: 1:3, 1:1, 3:1) on the growth of the diffusive layer was assessed by X-Ray Diffractometry (XRD). Outcomes illustrated that applying plasma nitriding causes the formation of the different nitride phases on the top surfaces of the M2 substrate and Fe3N is the dominant phase. The diffusive nitride layer has a higher current density based on simulation results. The microhardness of the surface increased remarkably and 2414 HV was reached as a maximum surface hardness. By considering the obtained results, 500 °C, 6 h, and a ratio of 1:1 of N2/H2 were chosen as optimum conditions of the plasma nitriding of M2 high-speed tool steel, and a corrosion rate of 1.62 mpy was calculated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: M2 tool steel
Plasma nitriding
Surface modification
COMSOL multiphysics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The current density distribution achieved from COMSOL multiphysics for M2 substrate with nitride layer of a- 5, b- 2.5 and c- 1.25 mm.
a- XRD patterns of nitrided samples at different conditions & b-Thermodynamic parameters of possible nitride phases.

Variation of the crystallite size versus the process parameters, a-Crystallite size-temperature at constant time, b- Crystallite size-temperature at constant gas ratio, c- Crystallite size-time at constant temperature, d- Crystallite size-time at constant gas ratio, e- Crystallite size-gas ratio at constant temperature, f- Crystallite size-gas ratio at constant time.
Surface morphology of obtained nitrided samples, a-500413, b-550411, c-600431, d-500611, e−550631, f-600613, g-500831, h-550813, i-600811.
MAP elemental distribution analysis of sample 500611, which exhibits the uniform distribution of iron and nitrogen.

Variation of achieved microhardness according to process parameters, a-Microhardness-temperature at constant time, b- Microhardness -temperature at constant gas ratio, c- Microhardness-time at constant temperature, d- Microhardness -time at constant gas ratio, e- Microhardness -gas ratio at constant temperature, f- Microhardness -gas ratio at constant time.
Metallographic images of the diffusive (nitride) layer, a-500413, b-550411, c-600431, d-500611, e-550631, f-600613, g-500831, h-550813, i-600811.
Comparison of the polarization curve of reference sample with nitrided samples.
Fitted-Nyquist curves and b- Bode plots of nitrided and reference samples.
Schematic of the closing surface imperfection by corrosion products to avoid penetrate the corrosive agent.
Utilized parameters for simulation through COMSOL multiphysics.
Chemical composition of M2 high-speed tool steel.
Applied condition of the different samples under the plasma nitriding process.

head.ce:title: Study the variation of current density & corrosion behavior of modified M2 tool steel: Simulation & experimental

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Engineering, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Engineering, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgy & Materials Engineering, Iran University of Science & Technology, Narmak, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied & Environmental Chemistry, University of Szeged, Szeged, Hungary
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied & Environmental Chemistry, University of Szeged, Szeged, Hungary

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the simulation of the current density was carried out through COMSOL Multiphysics on surface-modified M2 high-speed tool steel. The results demonstrated that in the presence of the nitride layer on the surface of M2, the current density was increased remarkably in the nitride layer and M2 was immune, and consequently, it can be predicted that the corrosion resistance was increased. For verification, the surface modification through plasma nitriding was applied on the M2 steel corrosion resistance and was evaluated through polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in the 3.5 wt% of NaCl solution. The results confirmed the simulation outcomes; therefore, optimization of the parameters was performed. The impression of the variation of the process parameter of plasma nitriding such as temperature (500, 550, and 600 °C), time (4, 6, and 8 h), and ratio of inserted gases (N2/H2: 1:3, 1:1, 3:1) on the growth of the diffusive layer was assessed by X-Ray Diffractometry (XRD). Outcomes illustrated that applying plasma nitriding causes the formation of the different nitride phases on the top surfaces of the M2 substrate and Fe3N is the dominant phase. The diffusive nitride layer has a higher current density based on simulation results. The microhardness of the surface increased remarkably and 2414 HV was reached as a maximum surface hardness. By considering the obtained results, 500 °C, 6 h, and a ratio of 1:1 of N2/H2 were chosen as optimum conditions of the plasma nitriding of M2 high-speed tool steel, and a corrosion rate of 1.62 mpy was calculated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tool steels contain a broad group of alloyed steels, and due to their promising features, such as high hardness, excellent wear resistance, and stability under harsh working conditions, they are frequently exploited for shaping and machining. One of the most common types of tool steels is the high-speed tool steels, which are categorized based on the alloying elements. M and W tool steels are the most common ones, which contain molybdenum and tungsten as the main alloying elements, respectively (Liu et al., 2024). The working temperature of them is 1200–1300 °C due to their high rotation speed, and they can be used in different industries such as forging, machining, drilling, etc. In such applications, high speed can cause high shear stress on some areas of the surface which can result in severe friction and wear. Due to the presence of molybdenum and some other elements like vanadium and chromium, which have a great tendency to react with carbon and form a carbide phase, usually, surface heat treatment such as carburizing, nitriding, and boriding is considered a post-treatment in their fabrication procedure to improve their tribology properties and service-life time (Taghizadeh and Aghajani, 2021) (Naeem et al., 2020) (Hu et al., 2024).

Among the surface heat treatments, plasma nitriding has been developed recently due to some advantages such as shorter process time, lower distortion and slight changes in dimensions, obtaining homogenous layers in complex geometries, lower temperature, and controllability on phase formation (Abdi et al., 2023a) (Aghajani and Behrangi, 2017). Plasma nitriding was applied on diverse substrates to obtain nitride layers, and the effect of the diverse parameters was studied (Yazdani et al., 2014). For example, Ni et al. (2024) applied plasma nitriding and post oxidation on 42CrMo4 and evaluate its wear and corrosion behavior. By applying a plasma nitriding, formation of nitride phases by reaction of nitrogen atoms with the elements in the substrate, for example, Tekdir et al. (2021) and Yetim et al. (Arslan et al.) applied plasma nitriding on selective laser melted Ti–6Al–4V and 316 L layered samples to study wear and corrosion resistance. In another study, Sarraf et al. (2016) studied the influence of the plasma nitriding on closing of the crack network of chromium electroplated layer on H13 steel. Tabrizi et al. (2021) (Tabrizi et al., 2020) studied the wear resistance of plasma nitride titanium. Karaken et al. (Karakan et al., 2002) studied the effect of the composition of the atmosphere (argon, nitrogen, and hydrogen) of plasma nitriding on the AISI 5140 steel. Diaz et al. (Díaz-Guillén et al., 2020) applied plasma nitriding on D2 tool steel and increased wear resistance. In some other studies, plasma nitriding was used to form a new compound on the surface of the substrate, e.g. Kumar et al. (2019) applied titanium nitride surface on high-speed tool steel to improve the tribological properties such as wear resistance. In addition, applying of other types of coatings on M2 substrate was reported, e.g. Zhang et al. (2022) and Rousseau et al. (2015) reported the applying CrAlN and TiAlN, respectively, by plasma nitriding on M2 substrate. Although the number of studies of plasma nitrided M2 tool steel is remarkable in the literature, there are less attention to the corrosion resistance of the M2 substrates, and it is not well-studied.

The high-speed steels compose a very good corrosion and wear resistance. However, since high-speed steels are usually in contact with the cooling solution, and considering their high working temperature, failure due to corrosion and wear is inevitable in these materials. Therefore, surface modification must be carried out to improve the life service time. In other word, although wear and friction are the main challenges in M2 tool steels, corrosion as a phenomenon which is probable to happen within cutting, should be studied carefully. To the best of our knowledge, there are lots of studies that have been conducted in this field, however, there is a rare study using a plasma nitriding and comprehensive study of the corrosion to understand the corrosion mechanism. Therefore, in this study, in the first step, the simulation of the current density as the main responsible for the corrosion of the components was performed using COMSOL Multiphysics, and subsequently, the experiment was carried out for verification of the results. The parameters optimization including time, temperature, and atmosphere were studied on the plasma nitrided of M2 high-speed tool steel, and corrosion resistance was evaluated through polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.

Considering the thermodynamics of corrosion, free Gibbs energy and electrochemical potential (E) are driving forces to trigger the corrosive reaction. However, the corrosion happens with ions and electrons transfer. Therefore, in the most suggested equations for calculating the corrosion resistance or thermodynamics of corrosion, current density is the none-separatable term. The most important corrosion equations are Nernst equation (1), Butler-Volmer equation (2), Tafel equation (3), and polarization resistance (4), which describe the relationship between potential and current densities in various electrochemical processes as below:
1
E =
E o
−
R T
n F
ln
(
|
C
r e d
|
|
C
o x
|
)
2
i =
i 0
(
exp
(
α a
F
(
E −
E a
)
R T
)
− exp
(
−
α c
F
(
E −
E c
)
R T
)
)
3
R p
=
b
i
c o r r .

The simplified form of equation (2) is called Tafel equation (4) as below:
4
i =
i 0
exp
(
α F
(
E −
E 0
)
R T
)
In the aforementioned equations, i indicates the current density, i0 is the exchange current density, and R, T, F, and α imply gas constant, temperature, Faradays's constant, and Tafel slope, respectively. To predict the target behavior and feature, simulations offer theoretical models; therefore, the simulation of the current density distribution in a coated M2 tool steel with nitride layer was carried out using COMSPL Multiphysics software through equation (4), Tafel equation, by applying boundary conditions.

In this study, to investigate the current density distribution and its effect, different thicknesses of the applied nitride layer on M2 tool steel were considered, as a main factor, and the results were validated through experimental procedures of applying a plasma nitriding in different conditions and polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy analysis. A 2D M2 substrate with a dimension of 10 × 10 mm2 was modeled in three different types containing a diffusive nitride layer with a thickness of (i) 5, (ii) 2.5, and (iii) 1.25 mm. The thickness of the layers was considered augmented to display the simulation results easily. COMSOL Multiphysics version 6.1 was used to perform the simulation. The default module of primary current distribution was chosen to study the physics at the interfaces and simulate the current density, and boundary conditions were applied by considering the different electrolytes. The used parameters are listed in Table 1 .

The M2 high-speed tool steel was selected as a substrate in this study. The chemical composition of the substrate was determined by using a quantometer (Sahand Company, Tabriz, Iran), and is presented in Table 2 . The samples were cut in the dimension of 10 × 10 × 2 mm3. The surface pre-treatment before the plasma nitriding was applied on the samples by grinding using SiC papers (60–2000) and polished using alumina paste. Degreasing in the acetone and ethanol mixture was carried out in the ultrasonic bath and dried by air. Then samples were placed in the plasma nitriding container and prior to the process, the sputter cleaning was done at the Ar/H2 ratio of 2:1 and temperature 100 °C for 1 h to remove any remained containment and oxide layers. Then, surface modification was operated under different conditions by altering the temperature (500, 550, and 600 °C), time (4, 6, and 8 h), and the ratio of inserted gases (N2/H2: 1:3, 1:1, 3:1), which are presented in Table 3 . Additionally, the voltage and current of the treatment were changed within the nitriding process. The pressure of the container was vacuumed before the initiation of the process and then increased to 1.5–3.5 mbar by inserting the nitrogen and hydrogen gases. Subsequently, samples were cooled down under vacuum conditions. To prevent the edge effect, the samples are implanted in the secure steel mold. The schematic of the process is presented in Fig. 1 .

X-ray diffractometry (XRD) was utilized for phase detection by using a Cu Kα lamp, the wavelength of 1.54 A, 40 kV, and 50 mA and between 2θ = 20–80, with step size of 0.01° and scan rate of 0.5°. s−1 to determine the phase formation of diffusive layer (nitride layer) on the surface of the samples. To determine the nitrogen-diffusive layer and morphology of the surface was examined by using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The 4% nitinol solution was used as an etchant. Surface microhardness was measured by using a Vickers hardness MDPEL-M400 GL with an applied load of 50 g and 10 s dwell time. The microhardness measurement was carried out from 5 different location on the surface of the sample and the mean deviation was calculated for each of them, and reported. The corrosion behavior of the modified surfaces was assessed by using polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at room temperature using the iVium device. The polarization test was carried out based on ASTM G5-94 standard and open circuit potential (OCP) was measured first and then three-electrode cell including counter electrode (Pt), reference electrode (Calomel), and working electrode (samples) was used. Three tests were carried out for each sample to assure the reliability and repeatability of analysis.

The obtained current density distributions from the COMSOL Multiphysics simulations are presented in Fig. 2 . As can be seen, three different areas were considered including the M2 substrate, nitride layer, and NaCl 3.5 wt % solution and the intensity of the current density in each area was simulated. The first conclusion of the simulated data is that by applying the nitride layer the intensity of the current density in the M2 substrate is reduced, which leads to better corrosion resistance, which is consistent with the reported studies in literature for different substrates by applying nitride layer (Abdi et al., 2023a). In other words, higher current density causes the easy transfer of ions and electrons (charge transfer), and subsequent corrosion. Therefore, lowering the current densities in the M2 substrate by applying the nitride layer as a protective coating. Secondly, it can be observed that by decreasing the thickness of the nitride layer, the intensity of the current density is slightly decreased. This indicates that although the paths for charge transfer are reduced, the number of electrons is diminished too. Thus, the protection of the substrate was improved simultaneously with the protection of the applied nitride layer. Considering the physics of the corrosion, obtained results indicate that decreasing the thickness of the nitride layer leads to a slight decrease in the intensity of the current density. This observation can be explained by considering the interface effects and the distribution of electric fields. A thinner nitride layer may facilitate a more uniform distribution of the electric field across the substrate, thereby reducing localized current density peaks. Additionally, a thinner layer might improve thermal dissipation, further contributing to a decrease in current density intensity. Therefore, it can be concluded that the thin and uniform nitride layers have better corrosion resistance. Finally, by paying attention to the intensity of current density in the solution, it can be observed that by lowering the thickness of the nitride layer, the intensity of the current density in the solution is decreased around the substrate, and increased around the nitride layer, which implies the better protection of M2 substrate.

Obtained XRD patterns are presented in Fig. 3 , which clearly exhibited the difference between pristine M2 high-speed tool steel and nitrided samples. Based on the presented XRD patterns in Fig. 3, the Fe3N phase (01-083-0879 and 00-049-1664) is the determined phase with a correlated miller indices of (210) in almost all samples, the main peak has appeared at 44.38°. Other nitride phases such as (Fe, Mo)3N (00-048-1408), W4.6N4 (01-077-2001), Fe4W2N (01-088-0687), (Fe, W)3N (01-087-0421), and Fe4N (01-077-2006) can be detected. The correlated miller indices of aforementioned phases are (211), (111), (200), (200), and (211), respectively. The reason for the formation of different nitride phases is the high affinity of the alloying elements in M2 steel to react with nitrogen. The main reason for the formation of the other elements nitride like iron-tungsten nitride is the equilibrium and slow cooling rate, which prepare enough time for diffusion of elements and nitrogen atoms to form a nitride phase (Akbari et al., 2010). The higher amount of iron nitride phases is due to the dissolution of nitrogen atoms that first occurred in the ferrite matrix and with increasing the nitrogen amounts more than the saturation limit (0.1 wt %), the formation of iron-containing nitride phases happened. Fe3N, (Fe, Mo)3N, Fe4W2N, (Fe, W)3N, and Fe4N are the formed iron nitride phases, and Fe3N is the determined nitride phase, which can be detected at 2θ of 29.9, 38.2, 41, 43.7, 47.8, 57.5, 62.6, 69.7 and 77°, which is reported previously by Tang et al. (2019). As can be seen, a little shift can be observed in different samples, which is due to the diffusion of the other elements to the iron-nitride phase network through a substitutional diffusion mechanism and formed (Fe, Mo)3N and (Fe, W)3N. Moreover, it can be concluded that by increasing the time and temperature of plasma nitriding, alloying elements can diffuse up to the surface of the sample, react with nitrogen atoms, and subsequently, form a nitride phase. The lower intensity of Fe3N peaks in some samples can be related to the formation of other nitride phases (in other words, lower volume fraction) and/or the finer size of formed nitride phases (Kaba et al., 2022). Additionally, Yan et al. (2018) demonstrated that as the temperature of plasma nitriding increased, the formation of nitride phase also increased, particularly resulting in the formation of α′-Fe and γ′-Fe4N in the plasma nitriding of M50 tool steel, and Fe4N peaks can be detected in XRD pattern of 600811 sample.

The crystallite size of each plasma nitride sample was calculated by using a Williamson-Hall method according to the XRD patterns (Emami et al., 2023), and variation of crystallite size as a function of process parameters such as temperature, time, and atmosphere is presented in Fig. 4 . The smallest crystallite size was obtained 93 Å, while the biggest one was 208 Å for samples 600811 and 600431, respectively. The decreasing trend of crystallite size by increasing the time can be observed in Fig. 4-c and 4-d. The reason is by increasing the time, there was enough opportunity for nitrogen atoms to diffuse to the substrate and provide more nucleation sites. The important parameter is the atmosphere, where the variations can be seen in Fig. 4-e and f. According to Fig. 4-e and f, at the lower ratio of N2/H2 (1:3), there are not enough nitrogen atoms for diffusion into the substrate; therefore, the lower nucleation sites were ready and crystallite growth happened, which is in the consistency of the reported results by Mohammadzadeh et al. (2014). By raising the ratio of N2/H2 to the maximum of its value (3:1), due to the decreasing of hydrogen gas, the solubility and diffusion of the nitrogen atoms into the substrate decreased again, and subsequently, crystal growth occurred. The optimum ratio of N2/H2 is 1:1 where there is a balance between solubility of nitrogen (due to the presence of enough hydrogen gas) and diffusion of the nitrogen atoms (due to the presence of enough nitrogen atoms).

Scanning electron microscopy was exploited to analyze the morphology of the samples. As can be observed in the presented SEM images in Fig. 5 , the detection of white regions on the surface of the samples confirms the formation of the nitride phases (Tabrizi et al., 2021) (Nakasa et al., 2015) (Hacisalihoglu et al., 2017), and they can be attributed to the metallic precipitations. The amount of these white regions is augmented by increasing the process temperature, which denotes the increasing amount of diffused nitrogen atoms at higher temperatures, and following nitride phase formation. The Map elemental distribution analysis was also carried out, and the results of sample 500611 are exhibited in Fig. 6 . It is shown that the surface of almost all samples is covered with iron element and iron nitride phases are the dominant phase on the surface of the samples. Moreover, the achieved roughness of each sample is different, and more smooth surfaces were obtained in samples with higher N2/H2 ratio, which can influence the resistance to scratching too (Moreno-Bárcenas et al., 2019). Additionally, the formation of cracks can be detected in the presented SEM images in Fig. 5. The formation of cracks can be assigned to two main reasons within the plasma nitriding, which are previously presented by El-Hossary et al. (2015). The first one is the higher heat input within the process. The second one is the distortion of the atoms network due to the diffusion of nitrogen and subsequent induced stress, which causes the crack nucleation and propagation.

The measured surface microhardness of plasma nitride samples is presented in Fig. 7 . The maximum and minimum values are obtained in samples 500611 and 600811, which equals 2412 and 825 HV, respectively. The microhardness of the reference M2 substrate equals 925 HV, and it can be seen in sample 600811, that the microhardness was decreased despite the plasma nitriding. The reason can be attributed to the activation of grain growth at higher temperatures and a longer time of plasma nitriding. Also, the M2 tool steels normally undergo temper-quench heat treatment. It seems that by applying plasma nitriding the tempering occurred once more and influence the microhardness value of sample 600811 negatively due to the high heat input value (this can be seen in OM image provided in Fig. 8 -i). On the other hand, increasing the surface microhardness can be seen in other samples, which is due to the formation of different nitride phases within the plasma nitriding plus, the thickness of the achieved nitride layer. Compared to the literature, it is shown that the effect of the temperature is much more than applying the quench-temper treatment on the samples before plasma nitriding. Araujo et al. (Araújo et al., 2020) reported a maximum of 1290 HV for plasma nitride samples at 400 °C. The effect of the variation of the process parameters on the value of obtained microhardness is also presented in Fig. 7. The surface microhardness has a direct relation with the thickness of the nitride layer, which can be obtained from equation (5) as below:
5
L = k
t 0.5
where, L indicates the thickness, k is constant and t presents time. It can be concluded that by increasing the time of plasma nitriding, the thickness is increasing and subsequently, the microhardness should be increased. However, as can be seen, increasing the time and temperature, almost have a decreasing trend on the surface microhardness, which can be related to the grain growth.

The cross-section metallographic images of the diffusive (nitride) layer are demonstrated in Fig. 8. It can be seen that in all samples nitride layer composes two different layers including white layer (outer layer) and diffusion zone (inner layer and blue one). Although uniform nitride layers with sharp interfaces can be detected in all samples, the thickness of the layers are varied due to the received heat within the plasma nitriding with variation of the process parameters, which confirms our observation in previous works (Aghajani et al., 2017) that by increasing the temperature, the thickness of the layer is increasing too and controlled by diffusion phenomenon. However, the maximum diffusive layer thickness was obtained in sample 500611, which has the highest surface microhardness.

The corrosion behavior was studied by conducting a polarization test in 3.5 wt % NaCl solution at room temperature for verification of the attained simulation results by COMSOL Multiphysics. The obtained polarization curves are presented in Fig. 9 . As can be seen, by applying plasma nitriding, the corrosion resistance is enhanced remarkably, and polarization curves are shifted to higher corrosion potential and lower current densities. This confirms the obtained results from the simulation and indicates the enhancement in corrosion resistance by reducing the current density. The enhancement of the corrosion resistance was reported before by Chang et al. (2014). In the simulation, the applied nitride layer was considered a uniform layer without any defects; however, as aforementioned, the crack formation happened within plasma nitriding. Although the formation of cracks occurred within the plasma nitriding, the corrosion resistance was enhanced significantly. This implies that the formed nitride phases are more noble compared to the available phases in the reference sample. Additionally, the fluctuating behavior can be seen in the anodic branch of each sample, which can be attributed to the presence of different phases in each sample. Additionally, the peaks in this anodic branch represent the activation of the pitting corrosion mechanism (Abdi et al., 2023b) (Abdi et al., 2023a). Despite this fluctuating behavior, no passivation behavior can be found in nitride and reference samples. The activation of the pitting corrosion can be attributed to the formation of cracks on the surface, where the corrosive agents can diffuse to the coating layer and get in contact with the substrate and interface of substrate and coating. Correspondingly, the polarization parameters including corrosion potential (Ecorr), corrosion current (Icorr), corrosion resistance (Rp), and corrosion rate were calculated through the Stern-Geary equation and results are presented in Table 4 . Furthermore, the corrosion rate was calculated, and as can be seen, the corrosion rate was improved remarkably and decreased from 10.496 mpy for reference samples to 1.62 mpy for sample 500831.

For additional investigation of the corrosion resistance, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was carried out too. Analysis of experimental data can yield a full semicircular curve in the complex plane and provides an estimated value for corrosion resistance, R, and capacitance, C. However, in practice, obtained experimental data can rarely yield a full semicircle curve, and a part of the semicircular curve can be obtained. The fitted Nyquist curves are presented in Fig. 10 . Two different perturbations can be distinguished in all samples. The first one is linear with different slopes at higher frequencies and almost semicircle parts at lower frequencies. It is prominent that the superior obtained diameter indicates better corrosion resistance. Thus, considering the fitted Nyquist curves in Fig. 10, the effectiveness of plasma nitriding in improving the corrosion behavior compared to untreated M2 substrate was demonstrated, which confirms the simulation results. In other words, the Nyquist curve of untreated M2 substrate has the lowest diameter, which implies its lower corrosion resistance. Also, the largest diameter belongs to sample 500831, indicating its higher corrosion resistance among nitride samples. Additionally, the slope of the linear part at higher frequencies indicates the resistance of the double layer for charge transfer. As can be observed in plotted fitted Nyquist curves, it is obvious that the uncoated M2 substrate has the lower slope, which indicates its lower resistance to charge transfer and by applying the nitride layers, the charge transfer resistance was augmented and subsequently, the current density was decreased and corrosion resistance was enhanced. Reducing the current densities according to the slope of linear parts in plotted Nyquist curves approves the attained results from the COMSOL Multiphysics simulation. Furthermore, it is worth mentioning that the equivalent circuit of the nested model was used for the calculation of the impedance parameters and fitting of the Nyquist curve. In the equivalent circuit, R1 denotes the resistance of the electrolyte solution, R2 is the charge transfer resistance or corrosion resistance, and CPE is the constant phase element. CPE is used in the case where the capacitive behavior of the coating is not perfect, and the decrease in the value of CPE decreases with the reduction of defects Table 5 . Furthermore, the bode plots of the nitride samples are presented in Fig. 10-b. As can be seen the highest value belongs to sample 50083, equals to 1288 Ω at Log F = −1.5, and the lowest value was obtained 148.5 Ω at Log F = −1.5 for sample 550831.

Considering the obtained impedance corrosion parameters and their variation, it can be proposed that the dominant corrosion mechanism for improving the corrosion behavior is the remarkable increase in R2, which is related to the applied nitride coating (Shanaghi and Chu, 2019). This increase in R2 can be attributed to the formation of corrosion products at the surface defects and hindering the diffusion path of corrosive agents. When the nitride coating is applied, as aforementioned and confirmed with SEM images, it primarily introduces some surface defects and micro-cracks due to the plasma nitriding process. These surface imperfections typically provide pathways for corrosive agents to penetrate the coating layer and reach the substrate, potentially leading to hastened corrosion. However, over time, corrosion products begin to form within these surface defects and micro-cracks. These products act as a barrier, filling the cracks and blocking the pathways that corrosive agents would use to penetrate deeper into the material. This barrier effect is crucial because it prevents the corrosive agents from accessing the substrate, thereby significantly enhancing the overall corrosion resistance of the coating. The schematic of this clarification is shown in Fig. 11 . In other words, although the formation of cracks within the plasma nitriding, the entrance of the cracks at the surface as a pathway of the corrosive agents into the coating layer is blocked by corrosion products, and access of the corrosive agents to the substrate was avoided.

The simulation of the current density in nitride-coated M2 tool steel was carried out successfully through COMSOL Multiphysics. The results of the simulation indicated that by applying the nitride layer on the M2 substrate, the current density focused on the applied layer, and the intensity of the current density in the substrate is diminished, which can lead to the improvement of the corrosion resistance. Subsequently, the plasma nitriding of M2 steel was successfully carried out and the corrosion resistance of the achieved nitride samples was evaluated by using polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy tests for verification of the simulation results. Based on the achieved results, the optimization of the process parameters was done too. The temperature of 500 °C, 6 h, and the 1:1 ratio of nitrogen to hydrogen gas were selected as optimum conditions. Although the formation of cracks happened by applying a plasma nitriding, the corrosion resistance of the M2 high-speed cutting steel was improved remarkably due to the blocking of the opening of the cracks at the surface and avoiding the access of corrosive agents to the substrate and also, decreasing the current density. The results of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy showed that by applying the nitride layer, the paths for the charge transfer are decreased significantly, and subsequently the corrosion resistance is improved. The corrosion rate was reduced by 99%, and 1.62 mpy.

Vahid Ranjbar: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Arvin Taghizadeh Tabrizi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mohammad Roostaei: Methodology, Investigation. Sara Sheykh Germchi: Methodology, Investigation. Hossein Aghajani: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest regarding the research presented in this article. No financial support or funding from any organization has been received for this research. The authors also confirm that there are no personal or professional relationships that could be perceived as a conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
